author: @guineapigzed
text:
Gigantic waste of public money.
I’d prefer none.
I hate the government deciding where my money goes.
It’s part of the problem not any solution.
PS I printed a copy of the cup he used to drink pee from.

author: @JimSmith001
text: What are the copyright implications? Distribution of prints and files etc? Could you get into trouble for freely distributing files of statues? Thanks for great channel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no you can't there is no copyright on public statues - look it up!

author: @pauloneill369
text: I purchased the Miraco 3D Scanner because you promoted it and now you're promoting another one!  Are you being paid by Creality?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they gave me the scanner for free to run it through it's paces - that's it - but it is a nice scanner for sure - but then so is the miraco - they are different - you might notice i have a pc with the creality and the miraco is more stand alone - it's horses for courses really

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Very cool.

author: @eh1702
text: And…not to be too “dark”, but this could have a role in crime scenes. In certain situations it could preserve and reproduce information that has to be disrupted.  How long did itbtake to do the actual scanning part of Ghandi?

author: @eh1702
text: I have been wondering how well the software can alter things that you scan. As a test, would you be able to do something like bend/straighten a limb of a scanned statue, and tweak things like sleeves? (It isn’t really statues I have in mind, but it would be a great analogue.)

author: @alden1132
text: So cool! Have you tested the resolution of scanning small items? I've been thinking of getting into 3D printing as a solution to a problem acquiring a machined part I have to order from Australia. The creator also only makes a limited number, more as a favor to the community than as a profit-making venture, so my projects are severely limited by availability of that part. The creator has made it clear he would be thrilled if someone else would make the parts, and encourages others to make them for themselves. The problem is that he uses a metal lathe, which I lack. I COULD, however, use the lost-PLA casting method to make acceptably accurate facsimiles for a fraction of the cost/effort of buying a metal lathe and learning how to use it. Hence, my question of resolution/accuracy of scanning small parts? The part in question is essentially a 6.5" torus with tapered/beveled edges.

author: @chrissscottt
text: How about a Robert Murray-Smith scan? Could coat them with copper and sell them as merch.

author: @De_Raaf
text: Rob Have you tried scanning something like an M12 bolt, printing it and then trying it in a factory manufactured nut? Be interesting to see how the scanner handles dimensions.

author: @frankfaubert1927
text: Great video. Does personal use like this violate any copyright laws? I wouldn't think it should and might fall under fair use laws. I mean, it's not like you're selling them down on the corner or anything.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - copyright doesn't apply to public sculpture

replies.author: @frankfaubert1927
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks Rob. I wouldn't want to have to set up a GoFundMe to get our favourite YouTuber bailed out lol.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I don't think I would be in trouble myself mate as I always just give stuff away lol -but if someone did want to sell models of it - they can and as far as I am concerned they are more than welcome to use my scan to do it

author: @vitordelima
text: Maybe a photogrammetry app from some smartphone is enough to scan many of those models.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try it and see

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringI can't.

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: @vitordelima I have an android app called Polycam (since there's no iPhone IR laser point cloud projector on my phone) but it does give pretty good results with most objects and lets you export to STL, and a variety of other 3D and point cloud formats. Of course you can't expect the same level of accuracy and flexibility as a dedicated scanner but it does work.
